title: Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita, 2020
The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) and GDP per capita for countries in 2020, with circle sizes representing population. The vertical axis measures the AHDI score from 0 to 0.8, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale ranging from $1,000 to $100,000. A positive correlation is visible across the data points, where higher income levels generally coincide with higher development scores. Countries in the lower-left quadrant, such as Burundi, DR Congo, and Chad, show AHDI scores below 0.2 and GDP per capita under $2,000. Moving toward the center of the distribution, nations like India, Pakistan, and Indonesia cluster around the $5,000 to $10,000 income mark with AHDI scores between 0.3 and 0.5. In the upper-right section, high-income economies including Switzerland, Japan, and the United States display AHDI scores exceeding 0.7. Specific outliers are visible within the trend; Qatar and Kuwait appear at the far right with GDP per capita figures above $50,000 but AHDI scores near 0.4, while Equatorial Guinea sits at a similar income level with a score near 0.2. Conversely, countries like Moldova and Peru achieve AHDI scores around 0.5 with GDP per capita figures closer to $10,000. The color-coded regions show European and North American nations concentrated in the top-right, while African nations are clustered in the bottom-left. The data shows a general upward trend where increases in GDP per capita align with increases in the Augmented Human Development Index.
